'''Keijo Erik''' "'''Keke'''" '''Rosberg''' ({{IPA|fi|ˈkeke ˈruːsbæri|-|Fi-Keke_Rosberg.ogg}}; born 6 December 1948) is a Finnish former [[racing driver]] and [[motorsport]] executive, who competed in [[Formula One]] from {{F1|1978}} to {{F1|1986}}. Rosberg won the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] in {{F1|1982}} with [[Williams Racing|Williams]], and won five [[Formula One Grands Prix|Grands Prix]] across nine seasons.

Born in [[Sweden]] and raised in [[Finland]], Rosberg started his racing career in [[kart racing|karting]] before graduating to [[Formula Vee]] in 1972. Upon winning Finnish Championship the following year, Rosberg progressed to [[Formula Super Vee]], where he won the [[Formula Super Vee#Formel Super Vau GTX (Germany)/German Formula Super Vee Championship|German Championship]] in 1975. He then moved to [[European Formula Two]], competing from [[1976 European Formula Two season|1976]] to [[1979 European Formula Two season|1979]]. Aged 29, Rosberg made his Formula One debut for [[Theodore Racing|Theodore]] at the [[1978 South African Grand Prix]]. He spent the remainder of the {{F1|1978}} season with Theodore and [[ATS (wheels)|ATS]], winning the [[Glossary of motorsport terms#N|non-championship]] [[1978 BRDC International Trophy|BRDC International Trophy]] with the former in his second Formula One appearance. Rosberg returned in {{F1|1979}} with [[Walter Wolf Racing|Wolf]], replacing the retired [[James Hunt]] from the {{F1GP|1979|French}} onwards. After another [[Glossary of motorsport terms#N|non-classified]] championship finish, Rosberg signed for [[Fittipaldi Automotive|Fittipaldi]] in {{F1|1980}} to partner [[Emerson Fittipaldi]], scoring his maiden points and podium finish [[1980 Argentine Grand Prix|on debut]].

After two years with Fittipaldi, Rosberg signed for [[Williams Racing|Williams]] in {{F1|1982}}. He secured his maiden victory during his first season with the team—at the {{F1GP|1982|Swiss}}—and his five further podiums saw him clinch the title at the [[1982 Caesars Palace Grand Prix|final race of the season]], becoming the [[Formula One drivers from Finland|first World Drivers' Champion from Finland]]. Rosberg was unable to defend his title in {{F1|1983}} as Williams struggled to adapt to the [[History of Formula One#1.5-litre turbo-charged engines (1983–1988)|turbo era]], despite winning the {{F1GP|1983|Monaco}} and the [[1983 Race of Champions|final non-championship Race of Champions]]. He took further wins for Williams at the {{F1GP|1984|Dallas}} in {{F1|1984}}, and the [[1985 Detroit Grand Prix|Detroit]] and [[1985 Australian Grand Prix|Australian]] Grands Prix in {{F1|1985}}, finishing third in the latter championship. Moving to reigning champions [[McLaren]] in {{F1|1986}} to partner [[Alain Prost]], Rosberg was unable to win all year as his teammate took the title, retiring at the end of the season with five race wins, five pole positions, three fastest laps and 17 podiums. Outside of Formula One, Rosberg achieved multiple race wins in the [[World Sportscar Championship]] with [[Peugeot Sport|Peugeot]] from [[1990 World Sportscar Championship|1990]] to [[1991 World Sportscar Championship|1991]], and was a race-winner in the [[Deutsche Tourenwagen Meisterschaft]], competing from [[1992 Deutsche Tourenwagen Meisterschaft|1992]] to [[1995 Deutsche Tourenwagen Meisterschaft|1995]].

Since retiring from motor racing, Rosberg has moved into [[sports management|driver management]], formerly managing two-time [[24 Hours of Le Mans]] winner [[JJ Lehto]] and two-time World Drivers' Champion [[Mika Häkkinen]]. He also coached and managed his son [[Nico Rosberg|Nico]] from karting at an early age to winning the World Drivers' Championship in {{F1|2016}}. Since 1994, he has owned and managed [[Team Rosberg]], leading them to championships in [[German Formula Three]], [[Formula BMW]], the [[Deutsche Tourenwagen Masters]], and [[Extreme E]].

==Early life==
Rosberg was born on 6 December 1948 in [[Solna Municipality|Solna]], [[Sweden]], where his father studied veterinary science.<ref>{{Cite web | first=Juha | last=Kärkkäinen | title=Rosberg, Keijo (1948 – ) | work=[[Suomen kansallisbiografia|National Biography of Finland]] | url=https://kansallisbiografia.fi/english/person/1934 | access-date=23 November 2018 | archive-date=24 November 2018 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181124055351/https://kansallisbiografia.fi/english/person/1934 | url-status=live }}</ref> Rosberg's father Lars Rosberg and mother Lea Lautala were both natives of [[Hamina]], [[Finland]].<ref name="Helmet">{{Cite web | title=Onko Keke Rosberg suomenruotsalainen? | work=Helsinki City Library | url=http://www.kysy.fi/kysymys/onko-keke-rosberg-suomenruotsalainen | date=25 September 2013 | access-date=23 November 2018 | language=fi | archive-date=24 November 2018 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181124055156/http://www.kysy.fi/kysymys/onko-keke-rosberg-suomenruotsalainen | url-status=dead }}</ref> The family moved back to Finland in the spring of 1950, originally settling in a [[Swedish-speaking Finns|Swedish-speaking]] village in [[Lapinjärvi (municipality)|Lapinjärvi]], where young Rosberg had language problems with other children, because his family spoke Finnish. The family later moved to Hamina, [[Oulu]] and [[Iisalmi]].<ref name="Helmet"/>

==Formula One career==
===Minor teams: 1978–1981===
[[File:Keke Rosberg 1979 Imola.jpg|thumb|left|Rosberg in the [[Walter Wolf Racing|Wolf]] pits in {{F1|1979}}.]]
Rosberg had a relatively late start to his [[Formula One]] career, debuting at the age of 29 after stints in [[Formula Vee]], [[Formula Super Vee]], [[Can-Am]], [[Formula Atlantic]], [[Formula Pacific]] and [[Formula Two]], then "feeder" series to Formula One. He raced for [[Fred Opert]], his American patron.  His first Formula One drive was with the [[Theodore Racing|Theodore]] team during the [[1978 Formula One season|1978 season]].<ref name="formula1.com">{{Cite web|url=http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/204/|title=Keke Rosberg|access-date=13 January 2008|work=formula1.com|archive-date=17 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150117160530/http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/204/|url-status=live}}</ref> He immediately caught the attention of the Formula One paddock with a superb drive in the non-Championship [[BRDC International Trophy]] at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]] in just his second race with the team, emerging victorious after many of the big names had been caught out by a tremendous downpour. Rosberg was not able to qualify for a race afterwards, and was signed by another uncompetitive team, [[ATS (wheels)|ATS]], for three races after the Theodore team scrapped its unreliable car design. He returned to Theodore after they acquired chassis from the Wolf Formula One team, but these were also uncompetitive and Rosberg returned to ATS to end the season.

Rosberg next emerged with the [[Walter Wolf Racing|Wolf]] team, midway through the [[1979 Formula One season|1979 season]]. However, the team was having difficulty staying solvent, and Rosberg had problems in finishing races. Rosberg soon had to change teams again when Wolf left Formula One, and signed with [[Fittipaldi Automotive]] which had bought the remains of Walter Wolf's squad. He secured his first two point-scoring results in the [[1980 Formula One season|1980 season]], including a sensational podium at the season-opening race at Buenos Aires, but the uncompetitiveness of the Fittipaldi car meant that Rosberg often failed to finish or qualify. 1981 was worse as he failed to score at all.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sky Sports F1 1981 Championship Overview |url=https://www.skysports.com/f1/stats/1981/drivers |access-date=25 October 2024 |website=Sky Sports}}</ref>

===The sharp end – Williams: 1982–1985===
[[File:Keke Rosberg Williams FW09 1984 Dallas F1.jpg|thumb|left|Rosberg won the [[1984 Dallas Grand Prix]] in a [[Williams FW09]].]]
Despite this, [[WilliamsF1|Williams]] was interested in Rosberg, with the retirement of {{f1|1980}} World Champion [[Alan Jones (Formula 1)|Alan Jones]] leaving a seat open for the [[1982 Formula One season|1982 season]].<ref name="formula1.com"/> Given a competitive car, Rosberg had a highly successful year. He consistently scored points and earned his first victory in the [[1982 Swiss Grand Prix|Swiss Grand Prix]] at [[Dijon-Prenois]] late that year<ref>Winner's Circle Swiss Grand Prix, John Blakemore Photograph Collection, Revs Institute, [https://library.revsinstitute.org/digital/custom/single-image?id=508722&collection=p17257coll1 Revs Digital Library.] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030022016/https://library.revsinstitute.org/digital/custom/single-image?id=508722&collection=p17257coll1 |date=30 October 2020 }}</ref> (despite being called the "[[Swiss Grand Prix]]", the race was held in [[France]] due to [[Switzerland]]'s ban on motor racing in effect since the [[1955 Le Mans disaster]]).

In a year where no driver won more than two races, with [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]]'s season marred by the death of [[Gilles Villeneuve]] at [[1982 Belgian Grand Prix|Zolder]] and the career-ending injuries to [[Didier Pironi]] at [[1982 German Grand Prix|Hockenheim]], and the turbocharged [[Brabham]]-[[BMW M12|BMW]] and [[Renault F1|Renault]] cars suffering from poor reliability (and not helped by Brabham continually changing between the Ford V8 and the BMW turbo), consistency won Rosberg the [[List of Formula One World Drivers' Champions|Drivers' Championship]]. This was despite his [[Williams FW07|Williams FW07C]] using the normally-aspirated [[Cosworth DFY|Ford DFY]] [[V8 engine]] which was considered outdated and out-matched against the vastly more powerful turbo cars. Rosberg won the championship with a five-point lead over Pironi, who had missed the last four races of the season due to injuries sustained at the German Grand Prix. Rosberg's 1982 Championship proved to be the last World Championship win for the old [[Cosworth DFV]] engine which had been introduced to Formula One by [[Team Lotus|Lotus]] in {{f1|1967}} (the DFY was a development of the DFV). To celebrate the victory, Frank Williams gave Rosberg two days off from testing and allowed him to smoke in the team mobile home.<ref name=TV218206>{{cite magazine | title = Äntligen finnish! | trans-title = Finnish at last! | ref = TVn21 | language = sv | magazine = [[Teknikens Värld]] | publisher = Specialtidningsförlaget AB | location = Stockholm, Sweden | page = 6 | date = 6 October 1982 | issue = 21 | volume = 34 | first = Fredrik | last = af Petersens }}</ref> As a result of winning just one race in his title winning season Rosberg equalled the record set by [[Mike Hawthorn]] in [[1958 Formula One season|1958]]  for the fewest number wins scored by a driver during a World Championship winning season, a record he still jointly holds with Hawthorn as of 2026.<ref>{{cite web |title=Stats F1- World Championship Titles-Wins |url=https://www.statsf1.com/en/statistiques/pilote/champion/victoire.aspx |website=www.statsf1.com |publisher=Stats F1 |access-date=29 August 2022 |archive-date=29 August 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220829182818/https://www.statsf1.com/en/statistiques/pilote/champion/victoire.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>

[[File:Keke Rosberg Williams FW10 1985 German GP.jpg|thumb|right|Rosberg driving for [[WilliamsF1|Williams]] at the [[1985 German Grand Prix]].]]
Rosberg's post-championship years would be hamstrung by both uncompetitive chassis from Williams, and the powerful but unreliable Honda turbo engine. For his title defense in {{f1|1983}}, Rosberg was again using the reliable Ford DFY V8. However, by this time, the reliability of the Ferrari, Renault and BMW turbo engines was starting to match their speed and power output. Rosberg still put his [[Williams FW08]] on pole for the opening race of the season in [[1983 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]] (where he was disqualified from 2nd place because he was push started in the pits after he was forced to abandon his car in his pit bay due to a fuel vapor fire), and then won both the non-championship [[1983 Race of Champions|Race of Champions]] at [[Brands Hatch]] and in [[1983 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]] thanks to a choice of slicks at the start when all others started on wets, but it was increasingly obvious that without a turbocharged engine, results would be scarce. To that end, Frank Williams concluded a deal to run the [[Honda F1|Honda]] [[V6 engine|V6]] turbo engine in his cars. Honda had come back into Formula One that year with the [[Spirit Racing|Spirit]] team and results had been slow with unreliability, but they were enthusiastic about joining Williams who had a reputation as a Championship-winning team. Rosberg and teammate [[Jacques Laffite]] first got their Honda turbos in the season ending [[1983 South African Grand Prix|South African Grand Prix]] at [[Kyalami]] and immediately the new [[Williams FW09]] was on the pace. Rosberg finished in 5th place to give him 5th place in the championship.

Despite the powerful Honda engines, Williams and Rosberg struggled in {{f1|1984}} mostly due to the FW09B chassis not being rigid enough to handle the power delivery of the {{Convert|850|bhp|kW PS|0|abbr=on}} V6. The Finn managed to tame both the car and engine long enough to win the [[1984 Dallas Grand Prix|Dallas Grand Prix]],<ref>Dallas Grand Prix, John Blakemore Photograph Collection, Revs Institute, [https://library.revsinstitute.org/digital/custom/single-image?id=521623&collection=p17257coll1 Revs Digital Library.] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030013309/https://library.revsinstitute.org/digital/custom/single-image?id=521623&collection=p17257coll1 |date=30 October 2020 }}</ref> but his only other podium for the year was a second at the season opener in [[1984 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]] (the third time in succession he finished second in Brazil, but the only one from which he was not disqualified). After a frustrating year he finished the championship in eighth place with 20.5 points.

In November 1984 following the Formula One season, Rosberg, along with fellow Formula One drivers [[Niki Lauda]] (the 1984 World Champion), [[Andrea de Cesaris]] and [[François Hesnault]], travelled to [[Australia]] for the non-championship [[1984 Australian Grand Prix]] at the [[Calder Park Raceway]] in [[Melbourne]]. Rosberg managed to qualify 4th in his [[Ralt RT4]] [[Ford BDA|Ford]] despite spending most of the day with fellow aviation enthusiast Lauda (his teammate for the race) attending an air show at the nearby [[Essendon Airport]]. After an early race dice with Lauda and a clash with Terry Ryan while lapping the young Australian which put him off the short 1.609 km (1.000 mi) circuit, Rosberg went on to finish 2nd behind the Ralt RT4 Ford of Brazilian driver [[Roberto Moreno]] who won his 3rd [[Australian Grand Prix]] in 4 years (having also won in [[1981 Australian Grand Prix|1981]] and [[1983 Australian Grand Prix|1983]]).

{{f1|1985}} would prove better for both Rosberg and Williams. The Finn had a new teammate in [[Nigel Mansell]] and the all [[carbon fibre]] [[Williams FW10]] chassis was a big improvement over the FW09B. For the first few races the team used the 1984 engines until Honda introduced an upgraded version which improved power delivery, fuel economy and most importantly, reliability. Rosberg used the new engine to good effect, winning the [[1985 Detroit Grand Prix|Detroit Grand Prix]] and claiming pole in the next two races in [[1985 French Grand Prix|France]] at the [[Paul Ricard Circuit]] and the [[1985 British Grand Prix|British Grand Prix]] at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]]. Rosberg's pole-winning lap at Silverstone created history when he lapped the 4.719 km (2.932 mi) circuit in 1:05.591 for an average speed of 259.01 km/h (160.94 mph). This would remain the single fastest lap of a circuit in Formula One until broken by Williams driver [[Juan Pablo Montoya]] at the [[2002 Italian Grand Prix]] at [[Autodromo Nazionale Monza|Monza]].

Rosberg's fifth and final Grand Prix victory came at the [[1985 Australian Grand Prix]] on the brand new [[Adelaide Street Circuit]]. As it was the final race of the season, it was also Rosberg's final race for Williams. Keke gave the winners trophy to his race engineer, [[Frank Dernie]]. The win enhanced Rosberg's reputation as a street circuit specialist, as four of his five championship Grand Prix wins (Monaco, Dallas, Detroit and Adelaide) had come on street circuits. Rosberg handled the 35[[Celsius|°C heat]] better than most and won by 43 seconds from the [[Equipe Ligier|Ligier]] [[Renault F1|Renault]]s of Jacques Laffite and [[Philippe Streiff]].

Just as the Honda engine began producing regular results, Rosberg decided to leave Williams at the end of 1985 and signed for [[McLaren]], winners of the 1984 and 1985 Drivers' and Constructors' championships. The Williams-Honda team would go on to dominate Grand Prix racing in {{f1|1986}} and through {{f1|1987}}.

===The final year – McLaren: 1986===
At the time, Rosberg's move to McLaren for the 1986 season had seemed a master stroke as they were the championship team of the previous two seasons, having done so (especially in 1984) in dominating fashion.<ref name="formula1.com"/> However, the 1986 McLaren was now somewhat underpowered compared to its rivals, and Rosberg, was soundly beaten by teammate, 1985 World Champion [[Alain Prost]] (the [[McLaren MP4/2|McLaren MP4/2C]] had been designed by [[John Barnard]] to suit the smoother style of [[Niki Lauda]] and Alain Prost, while Rosberg had never shed the [[Ground effect (cars)|ground effects]] style of late braking and throwing the car into a corner. It was not until it became known Barnard was leaving for Ferrari that the designer allowed Rosberg to fundamentally change his cars set up to suit his style. Ironically this coincided with Rosberg's only pole position of the season in [[1986 German Grand Prix|Germany]]). On top of that, the fatal crash of Rosberg's close friend [[Elio de Angelis]] while testing a [[Brabham]] at the [[Circuit Paul Ricard|Paul Ricard circuit]] in France in May 1986 deeply affected him and he retired at the end of the season. He would later claim that he retired "too soon" {{Citation needed|date=April 2010}}.

Rosberg dominated the final race of his Formula One career, the [[1986 Australian Grand Prix]], though he did not win. While holding a 30-second lead over [[Nelson Piquet]] (his replacement at Williams), he had a rear tyre let go on lap 62. Thinking the noise from the back of his McLaren was engine related, he shut the engine off and pulled off the circuit, only to find when he got out and checked that all he needed to do was drive back to the pits to change tyres. However, he later revealed that he would never have won anyway, that he planned to give best to Alain Prost in the Frenchman's bid for back-to-back World Championships (Prost needed to win the race with Nigel Mansell finishing no better than 4th to claim the championship, while Rosberg had dropped out of title contention some races before). As it turned out, Prost won the race and the title, and a lap after Rosberg's retirement Mansell suffered the same fate as his former teammate, though in much more spectacular fashion.

Rosberg, who had made up his mind in mid-1984 that he would only race for two more years (but did not announce it publicly until Germany 1986), had no regrets about leaving Williams and joining McLaren at a time when the Honda engine was starting to come on strong, while the [[Porsche in Formula One|Porsche]] built [[Techniques d'Avant Garde|TAG]] engine (and the 3 season old MP4/2) was starting to show its age. In an interview following his retirement announcement, Rosberg said that he was glad he left Williams when he did, stating that had he stayed with them he might have quit Formula One early in the 1986 season after [[Frank Williams (Formula One)|Frank Williams]]' pre-season accident (in which he suffered a spinal cord injury which left him a [[tetraplegic]]) had left someone in a position of authority within the team who he said was one of the reasons he had decided to leave Williams, adding "We simply could not stand each other". While Rosberg did not name the person, it was generally believed to be Williams head designer and Technical Director [[Patrick Head]], who had taken over the day-to-day running of the team while Frank Williams recovered from his accident.

==After Formula One==
[[File:Keke Rosberg - Opel Team Joest - Opel Calibra V6 at Melbourne Hairpin (32869941978).jpg|thumb|Keke Rosberg – [[Joest Racing|Opel Team Joest]] – Opel Calibra V6 at Melbourne Hairpin, [[Donington Park]], 1994 DTM]]
In 1989, Rosberg made his comeback in the [[Spa 24 Hours]] in a [[Ferrari Mondial]] run by Moneytron (cf. [[Jean-Pierre Van Rossem]] and [[Onyx (racing team)|Onyx]]), the same team that gave Rosberg's protégé [[JJ Lehto]] his debut in Formula One. Rosberg was a key element of [[Peugeot]]'s extremely competitive [[Sports car racing|sportscar]] squad in the early 1990s.<ref name="formula1.com"/> But after two years with the marque and varied successes (two victories and a failed attempt at the [[24 Hours of Le Mans]]), he moved on to the German Touring Car Championship, the [[Deutsche Tourenwagen Meisterschaft|DTM]], driving for [[Mercedes-Benz in motorsport|Mercedes-Benz]] and [[Opel]]. Here he set up his own team, [[Team Rosberg]], in 1995 and at the end of that year withdrew from driving to concentrate on running it.

Team Rosberg ran for another year in the DTM, until the series collapsed, and has been present in [[Formula BMW]], German Formula Three, the [[Formula Three Euroseries]] and [[A1 GP]] since. Team Rosberg returned to the revived DTM in 2000, entering two Mercedes. Success, or even just scoring points, became harder with each passing season and Team Rosberg quit the series after their 2004 campaign, only to return in 2006, this time with [[Audi]].

==Manager of new talent==
Rosberg later spent a long time managing his countrymen [[JJ Lehto]] and future world champion [[Mika Häkkinen]]. Until 2008, he also managed his son [[Nico Rosberg|Nico]] who entered Formula One in 2006 driving for [[Williams F1]]. In 2013 he and Nico became the first father and son to both win at Monaco, 30 years apart from each other. In 2016, he and Nico became the second father son duo to both win Formula One World Championships, after [[Graham Hill]] and [[Damon Hill]] had won the Championships of 1962 and 1968, and 1996 respectively.

==Helmet==
[[File:Keke Rosberg helmet 2017 Williams Conference Centre.jpg|thumb|Keke Rosberg's helmet]]
In his karting years, Rosberg had a white helmet with a blue stripe, then, in Formula One, Sid Mosca (who designed helmets for Brazilian drivers including Ayrton Senna, Rubens Barrichello and Emerson Fittipaldi) painted Rosberg's helmet white with a blue circle on the top, and the stripe was divided into a large blue rectangle covering the visor area with some blue rectangles behind (similar to Didier Pironi's helmet design). In 1984, the rectangles were replaced by a yellow trapezium. His son Nico used a design that had similarities to Keke's helmet earlier in his Formula One career, with grey replacing blue and with flame motifs, before changing to a new design in 2014.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=283_JhQChRQ| archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211117/283_JhQChRQ| archive-date=17 November 2021 | url-status=live|title=Nico Goes "Full Attack" with 2014 Helmet Design|last=MERCEDES AMG PETRONAS|date=4 February 2014|access-date=28 November 2016|via=YouTube}}{{cbignore}}</ref>

==In popular culture==

In level 7 game 11 of the video game ''[[Angry Birds]]'', created by the Finnish company [[Rovio Entertainment|Rovio]], there is a caricature of Rosberg in a racing car sitting on the year "1982".

In 1985, renowned Finnish actor [[Matti Pellonpää]] and his band released a song called "{{interlanguage link|Keke Rosberg formula rock|fi}}".

In 2020, he appeared with his son Nico in a [[Heineken]] anti-drunk-driving ad.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://adage.com/creativity/work/heineken-father-son/2240906 |title=Father and Son Formula 1 Champs Team Up to Promote Heineken 0.0 (and Send a Safety Message) |website=[[Ad Age]] |date=3 March 2020 |access-date=25 December 2020 |archive-date=27 December 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201227042851/https://adage.com/creativity/work/heineken-father-son/2240906 |url-status=live }}</ref>
